The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. full_notes:
Framing the Constitution is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 15.2 Images of desolation and destruction continued to haunt members of the Constituent Assembly.

The text explains that framing the Constitution The Beginning of a N The Beginning of a N The Beginning of a N The Beginning of a N The Beginning of a Ne e e e ew Er w Er w Er w Er w Era a a a a The Indian Constitution, which came into effect on 26 January 1950, has the dubious distinction of being the longest in the world.

The text explains that but its length and complexity are perhaps understandable when one considers the country’s size and diversity.

The text explains that at Independence, India was not merely large and diverse, but also deeply divided.

The text explains that a Constitution designed to keep the country together, and to take it forward, had necessarily to be an elaborate, carefully-worked-out, and painstakingly drafted document.

The text explains that for one thing, it sought to heal wounds of the past and the present, to make Indians of different classes, castes and communities come together in a shared political experiment.

The text explains that for another, it sought to nurture democratic institutions in what had long been a culture of hierarchy and deference.

The text explains that the Constitution of India was framed between December 1946 and November 1949.

The text explains that during this time its drafts were discussed clause by clause in the Constituent Assembly of India.

The text explains that 12.1 The Constitution was signed in December 1949 after three years of debate.

The text explains that a Tumultuous Time The years immediately preceding the making of the Constitution had been exceptionally tumultuous: a time of great hope, but also of abject disappointment.

The text explains that on 15 August 1947, India had been made free, but it had also been divided.

The text explains that fresh in popular memory were the Quit India struggle of 1942 – perhaps the most widespread popular movement against the British Raj – as well as the bid by Subhas Chandra Bose to win freedom through armed struggle with foreign aid.

The text explains that an even more recent upsurge had also evoked much popular sympathy – this was the rising of the ratings of the Royal Indian Navy in Bombay and other cities in the spring of 1946.

The text explains that through the late 1940s there were periodic, if scattered, mass protests of workers and peasants in different parts of the country.

The text explains that one striking feature of these popular upsurges was the degree of Hindu-Muslim unity they manifested.

The text explains that the Great Calcutta Killings of August 1946 began a year of almost continuous rioting across northern and eastern India (see Chapter 11).

The text explains that from your political science textbooks you know what the Constitution of India is, and you have seen how it has worked over the decades since Independence.

The text explains that this chapter will introduce you to the history that lies behind the Constitution, and the intense debates that were part of its making.

The text explains that if we try and hear the voices within the Constituent Assembly, we get an idea of the process through which the Constitution was framed and the vision of the new nation formulated.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 318 sudden death or the squeezing of opportunities on the one side, and a forcible tearing away from their age-old roots on the other.

The text explains that when the British left India, the constitutional status of these princes remained ambiguous.

The text explains that 12.4 The Constituent Assembly in session Sardar Vallabh Bhai Patel is seen sitting second from right.

The text explains that but the League chose to boycott the Constituent Assembly, pressing its demand for Pakistan with a separate constitution.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 320 counter-criticisms in the press in turn shaped the nature of the consensus that was ultimately reached on specific issues.

The text explains that many of the linguistic minorities wanted the protection of their mother tongue, religious minorities asked for special safeguards, while dalits demanded an end to all caste oppression and reservation of seats in government bodies.

The text explains that important issues of cultural rights and social justice raised in these public discussions were debated on the floor of the Assembly.

The text explains that in this capacity, he served as Chairman of the Drafting Committee of the Constitution.

The text explains that munshi from Gujarat and Alladi Krishnaswamy Aiyar from Madras, both of whom gave crucial inputs in the drafting of the Constitution.

The text explains that rau, Constitutional Advisor to the Government of India, who prepared a series of background papers based on a close study of the political systems obtaining in other countries.

The text explains that union Constitution Pandit Committee Jawaharlal Nehru 4.

The text explains that provincial Constitution Vallabhbhai Committee Patel 5.

The text explains that ambedkar himself had the responsibility of guiding the Draft Constitution through the Assembly.

The text explains that but while the process was long it was also extremely interesting.

The text explains that in their presentations we can discern many conflicting ideas of India – of what language Indians should speak, of what political and economic systems the nation should follow, of what moral values its citizens should uphold or disavow.

The text explains that the Vision of the Constitution On 13 December 1946, Jawaharlal Nehru introduced the “Objectives Resolution” in the Constituent Assembly.

The text explains that it was a momentous resolution that outlined the defining ideals of the Constitution of Independent India, and provided the framework within which the work of constitution-making was to proceed.

The text explains that as he spoke, he said, his mind went back to the historic efforts in the past to produce such documents of rights. “We are not going just to copy” This is what Jawaharlal Nehru said in his famous speech of 13 December 1946: My mind goes back to the various Constituent Assemblies that have gone before and of what took place at the making of the great American nation when the fathers of that nation met and fashioned out a Constitution which has stood the test of so many years, more than a century and a half, and of the great nation which has resulted, which has been built up on the basis of that Constitution.

The text explains that 323 FRAMING THE CONSTITUTION Then my mind goes back to a more recent revolution which gave rise to a new type of State, the revolution that took place in Russia and out of which has arisen the Union of the Soviet Socialist Republics, another mighty country which is playing a tremendous part in the world, not only a mighty country but for us in India, a neighbouring country.

The text explains that india is bound to be sovereign, it is bound to be independent and it is bound to be a republic … Now, some friends have raised the question: “Why have you not put in the word ‘democratic’ here.?” Well, I told them that it is conceivable, of course, that a republic may not be democratic but the whole of our past is witness to this fact that we stand for democratic institutions.

The text explains that in any event whatever system of government we may establish here must fit in with the temper of our people and be acceptable to them.

The text explains that well, I stand for Socialism and, I hope, India will stand for Socialism and that India will go towards the constitution of a Socialist State and I do believe that the whole world will have to go that way.

The text explains that cONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY DEBATES (CAD), VOL.I Source 1 (contd) Oath of the Tennis Court

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 324 Nehru’s speech (Source 1) merits careful scrutiny.

The text explains that what was he saying about the origin of the ideas embodied in the vision of the Constitution?

The text explains that in returning to the past and referring to the American and French Revolutions, Nehru was locating the history of constitution-making in India within a longer history of struggle for liberty and freedom.

The text explains that 343 FRAMING THE CONSTITUTION Credits for Illustrations Institutions Alkazi Foundation for the Arts, New Delhi (Figs.

The text explains that 10.6; 10.8) Collection Jyotindra and Juta Jain, CIVIC Archives, New Delhi (Fig.

The text explains that 11.15) Photo Division, Government of India, New Delhi (Figs.

The text explains that 12.3; 12.4; 12.5; 12.9) Nehru Memorial Museum and Library, New Delhi (Fig.

The text explains that 12.6) The Osian’s Archive and Library Collection, Mumbai (Figs.

The text explains that 10.9; 10.18; 11.17) Victoria Memorial Museum and Library, Kolkata (Figs.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III, Chapter 4: Framing the Constitution

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
